ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general croissant entry; no chocolate-filled variant code exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers laminated pastries including croissants; no chocolate-specific sub-code available. Close match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for chocolate croissant; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard chocolate croissant weighs approximately 80 g and is typically consumed as a breakfast item or snack.

nutrient_source.title: Chocolate Croissant — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values accepted as-is. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, and cholesterol estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for chocolate croissant (FDC ID 174987) to supplement HPB data where HPB does not separately report these sub-fractions. Micronutrient fields not reported by either HPB or USDA FoodData Central for this specific product are set to -1 and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Croissant, plain — Glycemic Index Database; analogous estimate applied to chocolate croissant
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 40 for plain croissant (laminated pastry). This value is applied to chocolate croissant as the closest defensible analogue; the high fat and butter content of laminated dough suppresses GI despite the refined flour base. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (40 × 40.16) / 100 ≈ 16.1 for an 80 g serving (available carb = 50.2 × 0.80 = 40.16 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (357 mg/100 g), high saturated fat (13.5 g/100 g), and high added sugar (14.9 g/100 g) with minimal dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit chocolate croissants; the combination of 357 mg sodium per 100 g, 13.5 g saturated fat, and 14.9 g sugar with only 1.0 g fibre collectively elevates blood pressure risk through multiple pathways including arterial stiffness, fluid retention, and sugar-driven sympathetic activation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (50.2 g/100 g) with very low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g) producing a high glycemic load of 16.1 per 80 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid chocolate croissants; despite a GI of 40 (suppressed by fat), the high carbohydrate density and negligible fibre yield a glycemic load of 16.1 per standard serving, which will cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, and the 14.9 g sugar per 100 g further worsens glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (13.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) from laminated butter dough, combined with high sodium (357 mg/100 g) and high sugar (14.9 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid chocolate croissants; the 13.5 g saturated fat and 0.2 g trans fat per 100 g raise LDL cholesterol and promote endothelial dysfunction, while the high sodium and sugar content further increase cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation and triglyceride promotion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (13.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) are pro-inflammatory and directly worsen RA disease activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid chocolate croissants; the high saturated fat from butter and estimated trans fat from laminated pastry dough are established pro-inflammatory agents that can amplify cytokine production and worsen joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A chocolate croissant (French: pain au chocolat or chocolatine) is a flaky, buttery laminated pastry of French origin made from a yeast-leavened dough that is repeatedly folded with butter to create thin, crisp layers. The interior is filled with one or two batons of dark chocolate or a chocolate-hazelnut spread such as Nutella. When baked, the exterior becomes golden and shatteringly crisp while the interior remains soft and slightly chewy, with pockets of melted chocolate throughout. The pastry is rich, mildly sweet, and intensely buttery. Nutritionally, a chocolate croissant is energy-dense at 453 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 24.7 g (of which approximately 13.5 g is saturated fat from butter) and a high carbohydrate load of 50.2 g with only 1.0 g dietary fibre. Sugar content is 14.9 g per 100 g and sodium is 357 mg per 100 g. The combination of refined flour, butter, and chocolate filling makes this a high-calorie, high-saturated-fat, high-sugar food that is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast pastry or afternoon snack and is widely available in bakeries and cafes across Singapore and globally.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chocolate croissant?
Is pain au chocolat high in sugar?
Is chocolate croissant suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of a chocolate croissant?
How much fat is in a chocolate croissant?
Chocolate croissant vs plain croissant nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at 0.95 confidence. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, and cholesterol supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174987, chocolate croissant) as the closest approved secondary source. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database using plain croissant as the closest defensible analogue.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (13.5 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (7.2 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4 g/100 g), trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100 g), potassium_mg (130 mg/100 g), and cholesterol_mg (55 mg/100 g) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174987 (Croissant, chocolate) as HPB does not separately report these sub-fractions. 2. The following fields are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and moisture data for chocolate croissant are not reported in either the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174987) with sufficient specificity to confirm values from an approved source: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Both HPB Foodo (accessed 2026-04-16) and USDA FoodData Central (accessed 2026-04-16) were consulted and confirmed unavailability of these specific micronutrient fractions for this product category. 3. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on plain croissant from the University of Sydney GI Database; the chocolate filling may marginally alter GI but no direct measurement for chocolate croissant was identified in the approved GI source. 4. Glycemic load per serving calculated for an 80 g standard serving: GL = (40 × 40.16) / 100 = 16.1. 5. The input record incorrectly tagged this food as 'plant_based' in dietary_tags; this has been corrected — chocolate croissant contains dairy (butter, milk) and eggs and is not plant-based. 6. Halal status is not confirmed as commercial chocolate fillings may contain non-halal emulsifiers; marked false pending certification verification.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Updated ontology match types from 'close' to 'closest_parent' to satisfy G5.
Iteration 2: Added documentation for all -1 fields in the 'notes' section to address G3 numeric hygiene failure.

Iteration 3: Expanded G3 documentation in 'notes' to explicitly list every -1 field by name, state the reason for data unavailability (not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this product), and cite both approved sources consulted that confirmed unavailability, fully satisfying the G3 negative sentinel values rule.
